>The second source you linked showed one kid it gave an age and that kid was 17

It had a bunch of kids. I can find others if you'd prefer. 

>cross-gender behaviors often start between 2 and 4 years old

Yes. However, most who at a young age have cross-gender or nonconforming behavior will eventually settle into their biological gender.

column7: Ok, so I found [a source](https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/five-myths-about-transgender-issues/2016/05/13/eca17dbc-177e-11e6-9e16-2e5a123aac62_story.html?utm_term=.584f8ec1add5) that says that "children as young as 2 can present with gender incongruence. According to the American Psychiatric Association, cross-gender behaviors often start between 2 and 4 years old. One study by the TransYouth Project found that kids as young as 5 respond to psychological gender-association tests, which evaluate how people understand their gender roles." The second source you linked showed one kid it gave an age and that kid was 17, which is only one year from full medical autonomy in the United States.

column7:
The heavy implication of "biological sex is a social construct" is that there are no significant inherent differences between the biological sexes, that they are made up by society. 

I would argue that biological sex is not a social construct in the same way you argue math is not. 1+1=2, XX=female, XY=male. Male's have sexual organs that produce seed while female's have sexual organs which produce eggs. That is not some contrived difference made up by society. That is not a social construct, that is tangible biological science.
column19: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

column7:
I'm not sure if this counts as a *social* construct, but biological sex is a construct (i.e. a general idea derived from specific instances), because it works with characteristics that fit in most cases, but not in all. It's an approximation.

For any physical characteristic you can think of, it's possible to find a man or woman who doesn't possess it. This means that no single characteristic can be considered essential/required/necessary to be considered a member of that specific sex.

For example:

* There are XX men and XY women
* There are men with breasts and women with adam's apples etc.
* There are men/women who don't possess the typical genitals for their group, or a set of both
* There are men/women who don't possess the necessary facilities to reproduce (including gametes)

Yet no one would say that these persons are not considered men or women, respectively. So at most you can say: in general/typically, women possess XYZ characteristics and men possess ABC characteristics.

column7:
My view is that the danger and the cost of sending humans, no matter how fast they are is outweighed by the advantages of funding further research into fast,adaptable, reliable autonomous robots.

 We have really good robots today. In the future we might have von neuman machines and gods know what. The effort to create better robots will create offshoot technologies which would benefit society at large.

And you will have to define progress for me. To progress is to make things better, I do not see how sending people to mars makes things better for people on earth other than providing extremely costly entertainment which could have been used to build better robots.

>Also I think the societal impact of having a human do the exploration is very important as it can inspire generations of motivated people to continue the work and advance society

Here is the problem with this logic. If going to mars is not the best idea, what is the point of inspiring people to go to mars? The argument kind of begs the question, it assumes that sending people to mars is already a good idea and thus people should be inspired to go to mars, when the very question is whether or not going to mars is a good idea in the first place.

I can use the same argument for space robotics and orbitals. The difference is these are better ideas to begin with (or at least that is my view). People will be inspired if they think that there is a reasonable chance they can live in space one day. 

>The average person doesn't really care or know much about Mars rovers but almost every can tell you Neil Armstrong's name

How many people know Eugene Cernan? Do you? He was the last person on the moon. Neil Armstrong was a once off and people forgot about the moon missions for the most part. People forget. They lose interest.

Mars Rovers were notable when they first landed but the missions were ongoing, so people inevitably forgot how cool it is that we are remote controlling robots millions of miles away on an alien world. Even you lost interest apparently. The news cycle moved on.

I truly doubt that nerds will stop wanting to build and control space robots.
column19: ['Has a knack for creating unique art installations', 'Active', 'Democrat', 'Supports policies on environmental protection and arts funding']

column7:
The robotic vs human explorer argument is a very nuanced and detailed argument with no right answer. Although you're right that robotic exploration is cheaper and safer, at current and near future technologies it's far interior to human exploration. The speed of a human explorer is just unrivaled. For example, in a single day on the moon, the apollo program astronauts collected samples and traveled more distance than opportunity mars Rover did in 8 years. A human with tools is just so much faster, more adaptable, and more reliable than a robot. 

This however doesn't mean it isn't dangerous. However, I think that the potential cost of human life is worth the scientific and cultural progress. There's plenty of people who'd be willing to die on Mars or go there without a return trip. Also I think the societal impact of having a human do the exploration is very important as it can inspire generations of motivated people to continue the work and advance society. The average person doesn't really care or know much about Mars rovers but almost every can tell you Neil Armstrong's name. I also think that exploration gives people purpose, it's an itch to scratch, and a robot just doesn't cut it.
column19: ['Practices yoga regularly', 'Active', 'Democrat']

column7: @camel-element I get where you're coming from, but let's be real - the risks and costs of sending humans to Mars are massive. Sure, humans can do more in a day, but the long-term benefits of advanced robotics can't be ignored. Plus, the inspiration factor is overrated if the mission itself isn't sustainable or beneficial. We need to focus on tech that actually improves lives here on Earth.
